hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Hominem quendam' — 'Homo quidam' is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Homine quodam' — 'Homo quidam' is nominative subject
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Hominis cuiusdam' — 'Homo quidam' is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'hominis' ends in -is

hints.to climb up: 'To climb up' is 'ascendere' — 'descendere' means to go down
hints.to abandon: 'To abandon' is 'deserere' — 'descendere' means to go down

steps.reveal: descendebat = he was going down / he was descending

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'descendit' — 'descendebat' with -ba- is imperfect
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'descendit' — 'descendebat' with the -ba- marker is imperfect

hints.ascendo, ascendere, ascendi, ascensum: These are the parts of 'ascendere' (to climb up) — opposite direction
hints.scando, scandere, scandi, scansum: These are the parts of 'scandere' (to climb) — the base verb
hints.descisco, desciscere, descivi, descitum: These are the parts of 'desciscere' (to revolt) — different verb

steps.prompt: Why is the imperfect tense used for 'descendebat'?
steps.target: it describes the ongoing background action when the robbers struck
choices: it describes the ongoing background action when the robbers struck
it indicates a completed one-time event
it expresses a habitual past action

hints.it indicates a completed one-time event: Completed events use the perfect — imperfect shows the action in progress
hints.it expresses a habitual past action: While imperfect can be habitual, here it sets the scene for the interrupting event ('incidit')
hints.it is a polite request: Polite requests use imperfect subjunctive — 'descendebat' is imperfect indicative narrative

steps.target: to fall upon / to fall among
choices: to fall upon / to fall among

hints.to cut into: 'Incidere' (from 'caedere') means to cut into — this 'incidere' (from 'cadere') means to fall among
hints.to begin: 'To begin' is 'incipere' — 'incidere' (in + cadere) means to fall upon
hints.to happen: 'To happen' is 'accidere' — 'incidere' means to fall among/encounter

steps.reveal: incidit = he fell among (a punctual completed event)

hints.present indicative: 'Incidit' can be present, but context (narrative sequence after imperfect) makes it perfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'incidebat' — 'incidit' is a perfect form here
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'incidet' — 'incidit' is perfect indicative

hints.incido, incidere, incidi, incisum: These are the parts of 'incidere' (to cut into, from 'caedere') — a different verb with the same present stem
hints.incipio, incipere, incepi, inceptum: These are the parts of 'incipere' (to begin)
hints.accido, accidere, accidi, —: These are the parts of 'accidere' (to happen) — different compound of 'cadere'

steps.reveal: in latrones = among robbers (acc. with 'in' expressing motion/encounter)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would also be 'latrones,' but 'in' + accusative shows he fell among them
hints.ablative: 'In' + ablative would mean 'among' (static) — 'in' + accusative shows the action of falling into
hints.vocative: Robbers are not being addressed — 'latrones' is accusative with 'in'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'latronis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'latronis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'latronis' ends in -is

translation: A certain man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho and fell among robbers
